mnemonics.title: Green IT Pillars
mnemonics.content: G-U-D-M: Green Use, Green Disposal, Green Manufacturing, Green Design - Remember: 'Using Disposed Machines Digitally'

mnemonics.title: Green IT Strategy Steps
mnemonics.content: A-D-D-I-M: Assess current state, Define goals, Develop roadmap, Implement solutions, Monitor & optimize - Remember: 'All Done Doing It'

mnemonics.title: PUE Meaning
mnemonics.content: PUE = Total Facility Energy / IT Equipment Energy - Lower is better! Think: 'Power Under Everything'

acronyms.usage: Metric to measure data center energy efficiency. Ideal value is close to 1.0
Power-saving technique that adjusts CPU voltage and frequency based on workload
Policy holding manufacturers accountable for product lifecycle including disposal
Discarded electronic devices requiring special recycling methods
Energy-efficient storage device with no moving parts
Harmful greenhouse gas found in old cooling systems
Measure of cooling system efficiency in data centers

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pizza: IT equipment is the topping (what you want), the entire pizza is total energy. PUE = Whole pizza / Topping. Closer to 1.0 means more topping, less crust (waste).
Think of a apartment building: One physical building (server) divided into multiple apartments (virtual machines) - more people housed, less infrastructure needed.
Think of a trash pyramid: Reduce (top, smallest) → Reuse → Recycle → Dispose (bottom, largest). Goal is to stay at the top!
Draw a circle: Power enters → IT equipment uses it → Cooling removes heat → Excess power lost. Green IT aims to shrink the circle.

analogies.analogy: Like a school bus that picks up students from different routes - one vehicle (server) serves many passengers (virtual machines) instead of many separate vehicles.
Like a television remote - it sends commands to the TV (server) which does all the heavy processing. The remote itself uses minimal power.
Like fuel efficiency in a car - lower PUE (like higher MPG) means less energy 'wasted' on non-essential systems.
Like composting food waste - instead of throwing away, materials are broken down and returned to useful cycles.
